skill.name: pyopenms
skill.description: Guidance for using pyOpenMS to analyze LC-MS/MS proteomics and metabolomics data with Python and OpenMS.
skill.summary: Use pyOpenMS for mass spectrometry file handling, signal processing, feature detection, identification, and quantitation workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are documentation false positives from Markdown code fences, scientific file formats, pyOpenMS APIs, and reference URLs. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, executable scripts, or malicious network behavior was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command findings primarily identify Markdown code fences and Python examples. The reviewed skill contains documentation snippets, not hidden executable hooks or shell command execution by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are fenced documentation examples. They do not define a script, tool entry point, or automatic command execution path.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Scientific Term False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections match scientific mass spectrometry and identification terminology, such as FDR workflows, peptide scoring, and file-format text. No cryptographic API use or password hashing logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context is mass spectrometry processing and false discovery rate filtering. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar cryptographic usage.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Findings Are Domain Metadata False Positives
low_findings.description: The certificate, key, and database detections refer to pyOpenMS parameter keys, protein databases, FASTA files, and sample metadata. They do not expose credentials, private keys, or bundled sensitive files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is ordinary API usage and scientific database terminology. No secret material or credential file contents are present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The network findings are static resource links to pyOpenMS, OpenMS, and GitHub documentation. The skill does not instruct the agent to transmit user data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are listed under Resources and are not used in executable code. No data exfiltration or background network call is described.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: A targeted review did not find instructions to override system messages, skip security review, claim pre-approval, or alter the evaluator role. No prompt injection finding is confirmed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed skill begins with normal metadata and a pyOpenMS overview. Searches for common injection phrases found no evidence in the scanned files.

content.user_title: Analyze Mass Spectrometry Data with pyOpenMS
content.value_statement: Mass spectrometry workflows require careful file handling, preprocessing, identification, and quantitation choices. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through pyOpenMS workflows for proteomics and metabolomics analysis.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to load and write mzML, mzXML, mzTab, FASTA, idXML, pepXML, protXML, and mzIdentML files.
Guides signal processing with smoothing, filtering, centroiding, peak picking, normalization, and deconvolution examples.
Shows feature detection, feature linking, consensus maps, and metabolomics-specific feature workflows.
Documents peptide and protein identification workflows, including FDR filtering, protein inference, and theoretical spectra.
Describes pyOpenMS data structures such as MSExperiment, MSSpectrum, FeatureMap, ConsensusMap, and identification objects.
Connects pyOpenMS outputs to pandas, NumPy, plotting, and downstream statistical analysis.
limitations: It is reference guidance only and does not include runnable automation scripts inside the skill package.
It assumes pyOpenMS and any external search engines are installed separately in the user environment.
It does not validate scientific correctness for a specific instrument, acquisition method, or laboratory protocol.
Large LC-MS/MS files can require memory and runtime planning beyond the examples shown.

use_cases.title: Explore Raw LC-MS Data
use_cases.description: Load mzML data, inspect spectra, summarize retention times, and prepare peak arrays for analysis.

use_cases.title: Build Metabolomics Feature Tables
use_cases.description: Detect features, align retention times, group adducts, link consensus features, and export analysis-ready tables.

use_cases.title: Post-Process Identification Results
use_cases.description: Read peptide and protein identifications, apply FDR filtering, map IDs to features, and summarize results.

prompt_templates.title: Load and Inspect a File
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner exploration of one mzML file
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pyOpenMS skill to outline the steps for loading one mzML file, counting spectra, checking MS levels, and summarizing the first spectrum.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Preprocessing Steps
prompt_templates.scenario: Select signal processing methods for a dataset
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design a pyOpenMS preprocessing plan for LC-MS/MS data. Include peak picking, smoothing, filtering, and parameters I should review before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Feature Detection Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use pyOpenMS references to design a feature detection and linking workflow for several centroided mzML files, ending with a consensus feature table.

prompt_templates.title: Review an Identification Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate my peptide identification pipeline using pyOpenMS concepts. Check FDR handling, protein inference, feature annotation, output formats, and likely failure points.

output_examples.input: User asks how to inspect a new mzML file.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan covering file loading, spectrum counts, MS level checks, retention time range, peak array inspection, and memory considerations.

output_examples.input: User asks for metabolomics feature detection guidance.
output_examples.output: A workflow covering peak picking, centroided feature finding, metabolomics parameter choices, adduct grouping, retention time alignment, and consensus export.

output_examples.input: User asks why peptide results changed after filtering.
output_examples.output: An explanation of FDR thresholds, q-values, score direction, protein inference effects, and checks for search engine score interpretation.

best_practices: Confirm whether data is profile or centroided before selecting peak picking and feature detection methods.
Record all algorithm parameters, input files, and output formats for reproducible mass spectrometry analysis.
Use indexed or streaming access for large mzML files to reduce memory pressure.
anti_patterns: Do not apply default parameters without checking instrument resolution, mass tolerance, and acquisition mode.
Do not compare feature intensities across samples before alignment, linking, and missing-value handling are planned.
Do not interpret search engine scores without confirming whether higher or lower values are better.

faq.question: What is pyOpenMS used for?
faq.answer: pyOpenMS provides Python bindings for OpenMS. It supports mass spectrometry file handling, signal processing, feature detection, identification, and quantitation.

faq.question: Does this skill install pyOpenMS?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users must install pyOpenMS and external search engines in their own environment.

faq.question: Can it help with metabolomics workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. The references cover feature detection, adduct grouping, retention time alignment, consensus maps, annotation, and table export.

faq.question: Can it process proteomics identification results?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers idXML and related formats, peptide hits, protein hits, FDR filtering, protein inference, and feature annotation.

faq.question: Does it support large raw files?
faq.answer: The guidance includes indexed and streaming access patterns. Actual performance depends on file size, hardware, and workflow design.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The audit found documentation false positives, not malicious behavior. Users should still review generated scientific workflows before use.
